title: L05.2.docstrings
body:
Toolkit for Finance and Financial Decision Making Lecture 5.2: Modules and packages - Docstrings

Breno Schmidt and Robert Tumarkin UNSW

There are two principle reasons to add descriptive information in your code:

1. Provide high level documentation that tells others (and yourself) what a module or function does. 2. Write comments as reminders of how statements work or the logic behind a complex algorithm.

“Code is more often read than written” – Guido van Rossum (Creator of Python)

Documentation is used to describe the use and functionality of modules, functions, classes, and methods.

Python documentation is usually provided through docstrings Docstrings are surrounded by triple quotes Docstrings are easily available to functions like help. For example: 1 >>> def add(a,b): 2 ... """ Returns the sum of two numbers """ 3 ... return a + b 4 >>> help(add) 5 Help on function add in module __main__: 6 add(a, b) 7 Returns the sum of two numbers

Comments are used to either explain parts of your code that may not be obvious or to keep other notes

A hash symbol # comments out code to the end of the line. Commented code is ignored by the Python. Inside string literals, hash symbols are treated as part of the string (e.g., "a string hash symbol #"), not the beginning of a comment

Modules: Docstring styles

We will use the NumPy docstring style

The NumPy Docstring Style is available from https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/format.html is both simple and widely used. To change the default docstring format in PyCharm and to take advantage of its code-completion functionality: 1. Click on PyCharm » Preferences. . . 2. In the Preferences window, navigate to Tools » Python Integrated Tools. 3. Under Docstrings, set the Docstring Format to “NumPy”

Ed contains a summary of the NumPy Docstring style for your reference.

There are there are other docstring styles, each with its own specific guidelines.

Here’s a NumPy style docstring for our withdrawal function. 1 def withdrawal(account_balance, withdrawal_amount, cash_in_wallet): 2 """ 3 Attempt to withdraw money from a bank account and update wallet cash. 5 If the `withdrawal_amount` is less than or equal to the `account_balance`, 6 the withdrawal is approved, and the amount is deducted from the account 7 and added to the wallet. Otherwise, the withdrawal is denied. 9 Parameters 10 ---------- 11 account_balance : float 12 Current balance in the bank account. 13 withdrawal_amount : float 14 Amount of money to withdraw. 15 cash_in_wallet : float 16 Current amount of cash in the user's wallet. 18 Returns 19 ------- 20 account_balance : float 21 Updated bank account balance after the attempted withdrawal. 22 cash_in_wallet : float 23 Updated amount of cash in the wallet after the attempted withdrawal. 25 Notes 26 ----- 27 A message is printed indicating whether the withdrawal was successful 28 or denied due to insufficient funds. 29 """ 30 <function_code_block>

Here’s a NumPy style docstring for our withdrawal function. 1 def withdrawal(account_balance, withdrawal_amount, cash_in_wallet): 2 """ 3 Attempt to withdraw money from a bank account and update wallet cash. 5 If the `withdrawal_amount` is less than or equal to the `account_balance`, 6 the withdrawal is approved, and the amount is deducted from the account 7 and added to the wallet. Otherwise, the withdrawal is denied. 9 Parameters 10 ---------- 11 account_balance : float 12 Current balance in the bank account. 13 withdrawal_amount : float 14 Amount of money to withdraw. 15 cash_in_wallet : float 16 Current amount of cash in the user's wallet. 18 Returns 19 ------- 20 account_balance : float 21 Updated bank account balance after the attempted withdrawal. 22 cash_in_wallet : float 23 Updated amount of cash in the wallet after the attempted withdrawal. 25 Notes 26 ----- 27 A message is printed indicating whether the withdrawal was successful 28 or denied due to insufficient funds. 29 """ 30 <function_code_block>

AI is very good at writing docstrings. The above was generated ChatGPT targeting NumPy style. 6
